summary:
Cromwell's Conquest of Ireland (1649–1653 AD) was Oliver Cromwell's military campaign to suppress the Irish Catholic Confederate forces and the royalist alliance that had supported [[event:2407|Charles I]] — a conquest conducted with exceptional brutality that remains the most traumatic episode in Irish historical memory and a central grievance in Irish-English relations for centuries. Cromwell arrived in Ireland in August 1649 and immediately stormed Drogheda (September 1649) and Wexford (October 1649), ordering massacres of the garrisons and, at Drogheda, of the civilian population and Catholic priests — actions he justified as divine retribution for the 1641 Ulster rebellion and as a terror tactic to break Irish resistance quickly.

The Cromwellian conquest resulted in massive land transfers: through the Act for the Settlement of Ireland (1652), approximately 11 million acres — two-thirds of Irish land — were confiscated from Catholic landowners and redistributed to Protestant English settlers and soldiers. Irish Catholics were displaced westward ('to Hell or Connaught' in the legend attributed to Cromwell), forbidden to own land east of the Shannon River, and subjected to the Penal Laws restricting their religious practice and civil rights. The death toll from the wars, famine, and plague of 1641–1652 reduced Ireland's population by an estimated 15–25%. The Cromwellian settlement created the Protestant Ascendancy that would dominate Ireland for the next two centuries and established the land ownership patterns whose inequity drove the agrarian conflicts of the 19th century — the deep roots of the Irish question that would preoccupy British politics through Victorian times and beyond.
